perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC, acting either directly or indirectly, through any other person, firm, or corporation, covenants and agrees not to (i) induce or attempt to induce or influence any employee of the Company to terminate employment with the Company when the Company desires to retain that person's services; (ii) in any way interfere with the relationship between the Company and any employee of the Company; or (iii) employ, or otherwise engage as an employee, independent contractor or otherwise, any employee of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of ten (10) years from the date hereof, ARC, acting either directly or indirectly, through any other person, firm, or corporation, covenants and agrees not to (i) induce or attempt to induce or influence any employee of the Company to terminate employment with the Company when the Company desires to retain that person's services; (ii) in any way interfere with the relationship between the Company and any employee of the Company; or (iii) employ, or otherwise engage as an employee, independent contractor or otherwise, any employee of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 10 years may be considered unreasonably long and unenforceable under Ohio law, as it could unduly restrict ARC's ability to conduct business.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in duration and scope. A ten-year non-solicitation agreement may be deemed unreasonable depending on the specific facts and circumstances, potentially violating Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 which prohibits unreasonable restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the non-solicitation period to 10 years, which the Perturbation Explanation states may be unreasonably long and unenforceable under Ohio law. The Law Explanation supports this, referencing Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02, which prohibits unreasonable restraints of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows Section 1331.02 of the Ohio Revised Code addresses combinations or agreements that limit trade or fix prices. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mention non-solicitation agreements, the Law Explanation suggests that a 10-year non-solicitation period could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under this section, making the change a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, (ii) call on, solicit or have business communications with respect to a competing business with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers for the purpose of obtaining competing business from such customer or prospective customer other than for the benefit of the Company, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of fifteen (15) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, (ii) call on, solicit or have business communications with respect to a competing business with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers for the purpose of obtaining competing business from such customer or prospective customer other than for the benefit of the Company, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-competition period to 15 years is likely unenforceable under Ohio law as it would be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in duration and scope. A fifteen-year non-competition agreement is presumptively unreasonable and likely violates Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02, which addresses restraints of trade.

Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the non-compete period from 5 to 15 years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that a 15-year non-compete is likely unenforceable under Ohio law, violating Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 due to being an unreasonable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 1331.02 of the Ohio Revised Code, prohibits contracts that limit or fix prices or lessen production/sale. While the snippet focuses on price-fixing, the Law Explanation extends its application to unreasonable restraints of trade through non-compete agreements, making the extended duration a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Ohio.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California, but keeping the original terms, could cause issues because California law is less favorable towards non-compete agreements compared to Ohio law. This is because California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements. By choosing California law to govern a contract with a broad non-compete clause, the enforceability of the non-compete is brought into question and is likely not enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that California law governs the agreement. According to the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, California law is less favorable to non-compete agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." This indicates that if the original agreement contained a non-compete clause (which the explanation implies), changing the governing law to California would directly contradict the intended enforceability of that clause, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ARC agrees that all copies and originals of all documents and other materials containing or referring to the Company's Confidential Information as may be in ARC's possession, or over which ARC may have control, shall be delivered by ARC to Belcan immediately, with no request being required.
perturbation.changed_text: ARC agrees that all copies and originals of all documents and other materials containing or referring to the Company's Confidential Information as may be in ARC's possession, or over which ARC may have control, shall be delivered by ARC to Belcan within 90 days of closing.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information by 90 days creates a window where ARC could potentially misuse confidential information, harming Belcan. This could be seen as failing to protect trade secrets, especially if immediate return is standard practice or legally required in Ohio.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 doesn't explicitly mandate an immediate return, delaying the return of confidential information could be interpreted as a misappropriation of trade secrets, especially if it creates an opportunity for misuse or unauthorized disclosure. The statute protects against the acquisition of trade secrets by improper means.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a delay of 90 days in returning confidential information, creating a potential window for misuse. According to the Law Explanation, this delay could be interpreted as misappropriation of trade secrets, especially if it creates an opportunity for unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" as including the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means, or the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. While the original text mandates immediate return, the changed text permits a delay, which could be argued as providing an opportunity for 'improper means' under Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61(A) and (B).
